Title: An unconventional proanthocyanidin pathway in maize

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: W23 (C1, R-r) background; BZ1 and bz1 seeds
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: PAs contribute to plant defense and have benefits for human health and ruminant nutrition; anthocyanins are seed pigments
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of bz1 blocks anthocyanin glycosylation/accumulation, greatly reduces cyanidin 3-O-glucoside, and redirects flux toward PA precursors and unusual procyanidin dimers in maize seeds
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bz1 encodes the anthocyanidin glycosylation step competing with ANR for common substrates. 2. bz1 mutant seeds accumulated much more epicatechin, confirmed as (+)-epicatechin, and showed strongly reduced anthocyanin content relative to BZ1. 3. bz1 seeds accumulated higher levels of iso-B2 and especially iso-B4 dimers, supporting metabolic competition between anthocyanin and PA branches.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (bz1 vs BZ1); LC-MS metabolite profiling; anthocyanin quantification; chiral-HPLC; procyanidin dimer analysis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a key competing step in anthocyanidin utilization rather than a PA-pathway catalyst; included because direct mutant evidence links it to nutrient flavonoid flux partitioning
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing anthocyanin glycosylation can redirect flux to PA precursors, but strong anthocyanin loss is a major tradeoff; may be useful only in targeted flux engineering contexts

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Maize varieties Suntava, Black Mexican, B104, bz1/BZ1 lines; tobacco cv Xanthi; Arabidopsis ban mutant
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Suntava and Black Mexican used for expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA starter and extension unit formation from anthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Predominantly (+)-epicatechin; also catechin at low levels; weakly associated with 4β-(S-cysteinyl)-epicatechin formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to flavan-3-ol PA building blocks, but in maize preferentially forms the unusual stereoisomer (+)-epicatechin and is inefficient at producing extension-unit precursors
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a bottleneck enzyme for PA biosynthesis in maize
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense; beneficial to human health and ruminant nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in developing colored maize seeds (14 DAP), with much higher transcript abundance than ZmANR2 in Suntava and Black Mexican
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in developing seeds of SbTT2-OX and SbMYB5-OX maize
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Endogenous ZmANR1 supports only low, unconventional PA production in maize, favoring (+)-epicatechin and unusual iso-B2/iso-B4 dimers rather than efficient PA polymerization
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmANR1 is the major expressed maize ANR homolog in developing seeds. 2. In PAP1-overexpressing tobacco, ZmANR1 caused only slight epicatechin increase and failed to generate procyanidin B2 or strong DMACA-positive PA accumulation, unlike AtANR or GmANR1. 3. In maize, elevated endogenous ZmANR1 expression correlated with accumulation of (+)-epicatechin, 4β-(S-cysteinyl)-catechin/epicatechin, and unusual iso-B2/iso-B4 dimers, but polymerization remained inefficient. 4. The paper concludes ZmANR1 is a bottleneck because it poorly facilitates extension-unit formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; heterologous overexpression in PAP1-OX tobacco; LC-MS metabolite profiling; subcellular localization; previous in vitro enzymology cited and integrated; chiral-HPLC-linked inference in maize
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Active flavonoid/anthocyanin background influences detectable PA output
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Inefficient PA polymerization and limited ability to divert flux from anthocyanins; unconventional products may hinder long-chain PA formation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: When ectopically expressed in Arabidopsis ban previously, only slight epicatechin increase and no procyanidin B2 were detected; product stereochemistry differs from dicot ANRs
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Native ZmANR1 alone is insufficient for engineering high-PA maize; should be replaced or complemented with a more effective heterologous ANR and possibly additional polymerization-supporting factors

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Suntava; Black Mexican; B104 transgenic lines
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Suntava and Black Mexican used for expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Predicted ANR homolog identified by sequence similarity, but no direct catalytic characterization in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense; beneficial to human health and ruminant nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Low expression in developing colored maize seeds (14 DAP), much lower than ZmANR1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in developing seeds of SbTT2-OX and SbMYB5-OX, but still much lower than ZmANR1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as a minor maize ANR homolog with low transcript abundance; contributes no direct demonstrated metabolic effect in this study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmANR2 is a second candidate maize ANR gene adjacent to ZmANR1 on chromosome 10. 2. Its transcript abundance is much lower than ZmANR1 in developing seeds. 3. It is induced by SbTT2 and SbMYB5, but the paper does not provide direct enzymatic or phenotypic validation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BLAST homology search; qRT-PCR expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is experimentally profiled in Results, though evidence for causal metabolic function is limited compared with ZmANR1
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low priority engineering target until catalytic function is validated

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Maize HiII crossed into Suntava and Osage backgrounds; tobacco cv Xanthi; source soybean cv Clark
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA starter and extension unit formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Generates PA monomer and extension-unit precursors efficiently, favoring the conventional (-)-epicatechin-linked route and promoting procyanidin dimer formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Acts as a key control enzyme when introduced into maize backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense; beneficial to human health and ruminant nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgenically expressed in maize and tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Heterologous GmANR1 increases 4β-(S-cysteinyl)-epicatechin and promotes conventional procyanidin dimers in maize with active flavonoid backgrounds, demonstrating that endogenous maize ANR is a bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In PAP1-OX tobacco, GmANR1 diverted flux from anthocyanins to PAs, eliminating red petal pigmentation and producing epicatechin, cysteinyl-epicatechin, and procyanidin B2. 2. In HiII maize alone, GmANR1 had little effect because the pathway was substrate-limited. 3. In Suntava and Osage maize backgrounds, GmANR1 strongly increased 4β-(S-cysteinyl)-epicatechin and the epi-cys/c-cys ratio, and increased phloroglucinolysis-released extension units. 4. ST-GmANR1 and OSA-GmANR1 seeds produced classical B2/B4 dimers and ST-GmANR1 also produced procyanidin C1 trimer.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in maize and tobacco; qRT-PCR; DMACA staining; LC-MS metabolite profiling; phloroglucinolysis; MS/MS
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires active upstream flavonoid biosynthesis background in maize for strong effect
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No major anthocyanin increase; in tobacco it reduced red floral pigmentation by diverting flux; in maize long-chain PA accumulation remained limited
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct evidence that ANR product stereochemistry determines dimer stereochemistry in planta
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering PA-enriched maize, especially combined with regulators that activate upstream flavonoid flux and factors improving polymerization
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Maize Ubiquitin promoter in maize; pB7WG2D vector expression in tobacco

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA starter and extension unit formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to conventional PA precursors and promotes PA formation in an anthocyanin-rich heterologous background
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense; beneficial to human health and ruminant nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgenically expressed in PAP1-overexpressing tobacco flowers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtANR efficiently diverted flavonoid flux from anthocyanins to PA production in tobacco, unlike ZmANR1
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtANR expression in PAP1-OX tobacco reduced red pigmentation. 2. DMACA staining and LC-MS showed accumulation of epicatechin, cysteinyl-epicatechin, and procyanidin B2. 3. This contrasted strongly with ZmANR1 and highlighted the unusual functional limitation of maize ANR.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous overexpression in PAP1-OX tobacco; DMACA staining; LC-MS metabolite profiling; qRT-PCR transgene confirmation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of red petal pigmentation due to flux diversion
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a dicot ANR benchmark rather than a main engineering target in maize
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Demonstrates desirable ANR catalytic properties for PA engineering, although GmANR1 was the enzyme tested in maize

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Glycine max; Arabidopsis thaliana
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Maize B104; soybean cv Clark hairy roots
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: PAs contribute to defense, human health, and ruminant nutrition; anthocyanins provide pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seeds, coleoptile, silks in maize; vascular tissues in soybean hairy roots
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgenically expressed in B104 maize and soybean hairy roots
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SbTT2 strongly activates PA precursor, PA, and anthocyanin biosynthesis in maize, demonstrating that maize retains responsive regulatory machinery despite lacking an endogenous TT2 ortholog
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SbTT2 activated the GmANR1 promoter in protoplast transactivation assays with GmTT8 and GmWD40. 2. In soybean hairy roots, SbTT2 induced DMACA-positive PAs and increased expression of GmANR1, GmLAR2, and GmANS, with strongest induction of GmANR1. 3. In B104 maize, SbTT2 upregulated ZmANR1/2, caused purple seeds, coleoptiles, and silks, increased total anthocyanins, soluble and insoluble PAs, and boosted epicatechin and cysteinyl-(epi)catechin by over 100-fold. 4. SbTT2 induced unusual maize procyanidin dimers iso-B2 and iso-B4, but PA levels remained relatively low overall.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transactivation assay; transgenic overexpression in maize and soybean hairy roots; qRT-PCR; DMACA staining; anthocyanin quantification; LC-MS metabolite profiling; phloroglucinolysis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates PA- and anthocyanin-related genes including ZmANR1, ZmANR2, GmANR1, GmLAR2, and GmANS
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: PA biosynthesis/polymerization-associated precursor formation and anthocyanin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major switch-like activator that overcomes the absence of an endogenous maize TT2-type regulator to induce PA pathway activity
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions with bHLH and WD40 partners in an MBW-like regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced seed size and seed weight in transgenic maize
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unexpectedly activated anthocyanin accumulation in a maize inbred deficient in C1 and B-peru activities, indicating conserved overlap between PA and anthocyanin regulatory networks
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulator for activating latent PA capacity in maize, but should likely be combined with effective ANR and strategies to mitigate growth penalty and improve polymerization
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Maize Ubiquitin promoter in maize; 35S promoter in soybean hairy roots

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Glycine max; Arabidopsis thaliana
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Maize B104; soybean cv Clark hairy roots
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: PAs contribute to defense, human health, and ruminant nutrition; anthocyanins provide pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgenically expressed in B104 maize and soybean hairy roots
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SbMYB5 activates anthocyanin and PA precursor accumulation in maize, but less strongly than SbTT2 for PA production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SbMYB5 activated the GmANR1 promoter in protoplast assays with GmTT8 and GmWD40. 2. In soybean hairy roots, SbMYB5 increased GmANR1, GmLAR2, and GmANS transcripts, especially GmLAR2, but did not generate obvious DMACA-stained PAs. 3. In B104 maize, SbMYB5 upregulated ZmANR1/2, induced anthocyanins and low but significant PA accumulation, and elevated epicatechin and cysteinyl-(epi)catechin less strongly than SbTT2. 4. SbMYB5 lines produced more catechin-cyanidin-3,5-O-diglucoside conjugate than SbTT2 lines, suggesting altered flux partitioning.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transactivation assay; transgenic overexpression in maize and soybean hairy roots; qRT-PCR; anthocyanin quantification; LC-MS metabolite profiling; phloroglucinolysis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates PA- and anthocyanin-related genes including ZmANR1, ZmANR2, GmANR1, GmLAR2, and GmANS
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: PA precursor formation and anthocyanin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally effective activator, but weaker than SbTT2 for robust PA induction
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions with bHLH and WD40 partners in an MBW-like regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced seed size and seed weight in transgenic maize
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Promotes stronger accumulation of flavanol-anthocyanin conjugate than SbTT2 despite lower PA and anthocyanin abundance
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for modulating flavonoid flux, but less effective than SbTT2 for PA engineering in maize
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Maize Ubiquitin promoter in maize; 35S promoter in soybean hairy roots
